cards.text: To create a 2D std::array with 3 rows and 4 columns: {{c1::std::array<std::array<int, 4>, 3>}} arr;

cards.front: How is a multidimensional std::array structured?
cards.back: As an array of arrays. A 2D std::array is std::array<std::array<T, cols>, rows> - the outer array contains rows, each row is an inner array of columns.

cards.text: To initialize a 2D std::array: std::array<std::array<int, 2>, 2> arr {{c1::{{{{c2:: {{1,2}}, {{3,4}} }}}}}}
To access element at row 1, column 2 in 2D std::array arr: {{c1::arr[1][2]}} or {{c2::arr.at(1).at(2)}} with bounds checking

cards.front: What advantages does multidimensional std::array have over C-style multidimensional arrays?
cards.back:
std::array provides:
  • size() method for each dimension
  • No array decay when passing to functions
  • Assignment and comparison operators
  • bounds checking with at()
  • Better compatibility with standard algorithms

cards.text: To iterate over a 2D std::array with range-based for: for ({{c1::const auto&}} row : arr) for ({{c2::const auto&}} elem : row)

cards.question: What type is 'row' in this loop?
cards.explanation: Each element of the outer array is a std::array<int, 3>, so row is a reference to an inner array.

cards.front: How do you pass a 2D std::array to a function?
cards.back: By reference with explicit type: void func(std::array<std::array<int, 4>, 3>& arr). Or use a template to accept any size. Unlike C-style arrays, no dimensions are lost.

cards.concept: Using type aliases for multidimensional std::array
cards.explanation:
Multidimensional std::array types can be verbose. Use type aliases for clarity:
  • Makes code more readable
  • Easier to change dimensions
  • Reduces typing errors

cards.front: Can you use multidimensional std::array in constexpr contexts?
cards.back: Yes, multidimensional std::array fully supports constexpr, allowing compile-time initialization and computation with 2D/3D data structures.
